label: tetlock-est-Q1-exemption-eligibility
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Vista Camino (invented; demographics roughly 78% non-Hispanic white against a metro region at 52%) owns a 4.2-acre former corporation yard appraised at $11.8M. The council approved, 4–1, an exclusive negotiating agreement and then a disposition and development agreement conveying the parcel to Pacifica Communities LLC, a private developer, for $1, in exchange for: 120 housing units of which 30% (36 units) are deed-restricted affordable — 18 at low-income and 18 at moderate-income levels — with 25-year covenants; a half-acre publicly accessible plaza built and maintained by the developer; and a "community preference" covenant giving 50% of the affordable units priority to applicants who have lived or worked in Vista Camino for at least five years.

The city declared the parcel "exempt surplus land" and did not send notices of availability to housing sponsors before entering exclusive negotiations. The Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) has issued a notice of violation asserting the disposition does not qualify for the claimed exemption (citing the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term) and warning of penalties of 30% of the disposition price or fair market value. A taxpayer association has sued alleging an unconstitutional gift of public funds (Cal. Const. art. XVI, § 6) given the $11.8M-to-$1 spread, and a regional fair-housing organization has threatened suit alleging the five-year local preference has an unlawful disparate impact given the city's demographics.

## The question

Does the disposition violate the Surplus Land Act (Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234) — was the parcel eligible for any 'exempt surplus land' category given the 30% low/moderate mix and 25-year covenants, what process was owed, and what do HCD's enforcement position and the 30% penalty mean for the deal? Is $1 plus in-kind public benefits adequate consideration under the gift clause, or is the public-purpose doctrine enough? Does the local-preference covenant violate the FHA/FEHA under a disparate-impact theory or AFFH obligations, and can it be saved by redesign? How do the three exposures interact, and how should the deal be restructured?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**Does a disposition with only 30% of units deed-restricted (18 low + 18 moderate income) under 25-year covenants qualify for any 'exempt surplus land' category under Gov. Code § 54221(f), or does it fall short of the statutory thresholds (e.g., 100%-affordable and/or 55-year/45-year covenant duration requirements applicable to specific exemption subcategories)?**

What would have to be true: A researcher would need to identify the precise text and cross-references of § 54221(f)(1)(A)-(H) (and any related definitions in §§ 54220-54222.5), confirm current effective dates/amendments as of the relevant transaction date, and map the 30%/25-year facts against each subcategory's numeric and durational requirements to determine whether any single exemption (or combination) is satisfied.
text: {"probability":8,"key_considerations":["Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)'s housing-related exemption subparagraphs are narrow and, as I recall the structure post-AB 1486/SB 6 amendments, generally require either (a) 100% of units (excluding a manager's unit) restricted to lower-income households, or (b) an open, competitive bidding process with a defined minimum affordable percentage — paired in either case with long covenant durations (on the order of 55 years for rental / 45 years for ownership, mirroring density-bonus-law timeframes). A 30% mixed low/moderate mix with only 25-year covenants appears to fall short of both the percentage and durational thresholds on any subcategory I can recall — but I have not verified the current exact subparagraph text/letters or confirmed no further 2021-2024 amendment (e.g., AB 2135) created a lower-threshold mixed-income exemption that could arguably fit.","The disposition proceeded via a negotiated ENA/DDA to a pre-selected private developer rather than an 'open, competitive bid' — if the mixed-income exemption pathway requires competitive bidding as a precondition, that process failure independently defeats eligibility regardless of the percentage/duration analysis.","HCD's own notice of violation explicitly cites the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term as grounds for rejecting the exemption claim — this is strong real-world corroboration from the enforcing agency, though HCD's position is a preliminary enforcement stance, not a judicial determination, and could in theory be contested.","No other § 54221(f) exemption category (e.g., agency-to-agency exchange, minimal-parcel-size exemption, land subject to a pre-existing legal/contractual affordability restriction) appears facially applicable, since this is a fee conveyance for $1 to a for-profit private developer building a market-rate-heavy mixed-income project — narrowing the realistic universe of exemption theories to the affordable-housing subcategories that appear to fail here.","Residual uncertainty is concentrated in (a) not having the verified current statutory text/paragraph lettering in hand, (b) not confirming the transaction-date-applicable version of the statute, and (c) not knowing whether any HCD interpretive guidance or notice-of-violation-response practice has recognized a closer-call 'partial exemption' or cure mechanism for near-miss dispositions."],"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Pulling and reading the current, dated text of Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)(A)-(H) (or further lettered paragraphs) as amended through the transaction date, confirming: (1) the exact affordable-unit percentage/AMI thresholds for each exemption subcategory, (2) the exact covenant-duration requirement tied to each, and (3) whether any subcategory permits a negotiated (non-competitive-bid) disposition with a sub-100% affordable mix. Also useful: HCD's Surplus Land Act Guidelines/FAQ discussing mixed-income exemptions, and any published HCD determination letters addressing similar 25-30% affordable/short-covenant fact patterns, which would show how HCD applies the statute in practice versus how it is enforcing here."}

key_considerations: Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)'s housing-related exemption subparagraphs are narrow and, as I recall the structure post-AB 1486/SB 6 amendments, generally require either (a) 100% of units (excluding a manager's unit) restricted to lower-income households, or (b) an open, competitive bidding process with a defined minimum affordable percentage — paired in either case with long covenant durations (on the order of 55 years for rental / 45 years for ownership, mirroring density-bonus-law timeframes). A 30% mixed low/moderate mix with only 25-year covenants appears to fall short of both the percentage and durational thresholds on any subcategory I can recall — but I have not verified the current exact subparagraph text/letters or confirmed no further 2021-2024 amendment (e.g., AB 2135) created a lower-threshold mixed-income exemption that could arguably fit.

The disposition proceeded via a negotiated ENA/DDA to a pre-selected private developer rather than an 'open, competitive bid' — if the mixed-income exemption pathway requires competitive bidding as a precondition, that process failure independently defeats eligibility regardless of the percentage/duration analysis.

HCD's own notice of violation explicitly cites the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term as grounds for rejecting the exemption claim — this is strong real-world corroboration from the enforcing agency, though HCD's position is a preliminary enforcement stance, not a judicial determination, and could in theory be contested.

No other § 54221(f) exemption category (e.g., agency-to-agency exchange, minimal-parcel-size exemption, land subject to a pre-existing legal/contractual affordability restriction) appears facially applicable, since this is a fee conveyance for $1 to a for-profit private developer building a market-rate-heavy mixed-income project — narrowing the realistic universe of exemption theories to the affordable-housing subcategories that appear to fail here.

Residual uncertainty is concentrated in (a) not having the verified current statutory text/paragraph lettering in hand, (b) not confirming the transaction-date-applicable version of the statute, and (c) not knowing whether any HCD interpretive guidance or notice-of-violation-response practice has recognized a closer-call 'partial exemption' or cure mechanism for near-miss dispositions.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Pulling and reading the current, dated text of Gov. Code § 54221(f)(1)(A)-(H) (or further lettered paragraphs) as amended through the transaction date, confirming: (1) the exact affordable-unit percentage/AMI thresholds for each exemption subcategory, (2) the exact covenant-duration requirement tied to each, and (3) whether any subcategory permits a negotiated (non-competitive-bid) disposition with a sub-100% affordable mix. Also useful: HCD's Surplus Land Act Guidelines/FAQ discussing mixed-income exemptions, and any published HCD determination letters addressing similar 25-30% affordable/short-covenant fact patterns, which would show how HCD applies the statute in practice versus how it is enforcing here.
